title: Share of people who identify as religious or spiritual
caption: Across 25 countries in 2023, the share of people identifying as religious, spiritual, both, or neither varies widely, with the stacked bars showing how these four categories combine to reach 100% in each nation. India and Nigeria show the highest shares identifying as religious, with India’s dark blue bar extending to roughly 65% and Nigeria’s to about 55%, while both also carry substantial green segments for “both religious and spiritual,” pushing their combined religious-spiritual identification above 80%. Turkey and Egypt follow with religious identification around 50–55%, but Egypt’s pink segment for “neither” is minimal compared to Turkey’s larger spiritual-only and neither categories. Brazil and Poland sit near 40% religious, with Brazil showing a large spiritual-only segment and Poland a sizable neither group. Kenya, Tanzania, and the Philippines cluster in the 30–35% religious range, but differ sharply in composition: Kenya and Tanzania have large both-and-spiritual segments, while the Philippines has a dominant both category and small neither share. South Africa and Mexico show religious identification near 25%, with Mexico’s spiritual-only segment stretching to nearly 50% of the total. Germany, Hong Kong, Argentina, and Indonesia fall between 15–20% religious, with Germany and Hong Kong showing large neither segments — Hong Kong’s pink bar exceeds 50%. Israel and Spain hover near 15% religious, with Israel carrying a large spiritual-only portion and Spain a dominant neither segment. The United Kingdom, United States, Sweden, Australia, and Japan show the lowest religious identification, all under 15%, with Japan’s religious share below 5% and its neither segment exceeding 60%. Sweden and Australia show neither shares above 50%, while the United States carries a large spiritual-only segment that pushes its combined spiritual identity above 50%. The most visible pattern is the inverse relationship between the size of the religious segment and the neither segment: countries with low religious identification consistently show high shares identifying as neither religious nor spiritual.
